narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate background, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection from our law school admissions process—it's a tough moment, and we're here to help you make sense of it. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 20, lower-middle family income, full-time student status, and school deciles of 3rd in both your first and third years, which placed you in the lower third of your peers academically. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students, predicted a 46% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try—a risk level that unfortunately tipped the scales toward rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which stands well below the typical applicant's score of around 37 and signaled potential challenges in the rigorous legal reasoning needed for the bar. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though below the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor by suggesting stronger academic resilience than the model initially expected. Your lower-middle family income also helped nudge the prediction toward passing, as students from similar backgrounds have often shown determination and grit in overcoming hurdles. Meanwhile, your lower deciles in early school grades provided a slight positive counterbalance, indicating some consistency despite not being at the top of your class, and your full-time enrollment aligned with the vast majority of successful students. Compared to typical admitted applicants, your LSAT was the biggest outlier dragging the prediction down, while your other factors were closer to average but couldn't fully offset it. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations have thrived with targeted preparation. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, as it's the top lever for changing the model's outlook, and perhaps strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or highlighting any upward trends in your record. Consider gaining practical experience, like internships, to build a more compelling narrative for future applications. You're capable of turning this around—reach out to admissions counselors for personalized advice, and keep pursuing your passion for law. We wish you the best on your next steps.
